original_hypothesis.title: FGenesis.createGenesis Permissionless Grants BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: FGenesis.createGenesis has no access control - any address that pays the fee can call it. Each call deploys a new Genesis contract and grants it BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3 via grantRole. FGenesis has DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3 (confirmed by deployment script). This means any fee-paying user can spawn unlimited Genesis contracts each holding BONDING_ROLE, expanding the attack surface on AgentFactoryV3 and potentially exhausting role management.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While Genesis contracts are constrained by FACTORY_ROLE for their critical functions, the unlimited granting of BONDING_ROLE to new contracts without access control violates least-privilege. If AgentFactoryV3 has additional BONDING_ROLE-gated functions, each Genesis contract could potentially abuse them.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: genesis/deployGenesis.ts grants DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactory to FGenesis

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control modifier on createGenesis; grants BONDING_ROLE to new contract via AgentFactoryV3.grantRole

preconditions: Attacker has enough VIRTUAL tokens to pay creation fee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No role check on createGenesis; unlimited BONDING_ROLE grants

operational_questions: What functions are gated by BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3?
Can Genesis contracts directly call AgentFactoryV3 BONDING_ROLE functions outside of onGenesisSuccess?
What is the creation fee amount vs potential exploit value?
validation_requirements: Review full AgentFactoryV3 for all BONDING_ROLE-gated functions
Verify Genesis contracts cannot directly call agentFactory beyond onGenesisSuccess
Test mass creation of Genesis contracts and role enumeration

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Pay creation fee to spawn Genesis contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Genesis contract receives BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If AgentFactoryV3 has exploitable BONDING_ROLE functions, call them from Genesis
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat to create multiple attack vectors

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Potential access to AgentFactoryV3 BONDING_ROLE functions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unlimited role grants expanding attack surface
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot easily revoke BONDING_ROLE from all created contracts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High volume of createGenesis calls from single address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpectedly many BONDING_ROLE holders on AgentFactory

validation_result.impact_summary: Any fee-paying user can create unlimited Genesis contracts, each automatically granted BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3, expanding the privileged attack surface with no access control or bound.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The FGenesis.createGenesis function (FGenesis.sol lines 63-82) has no access control modifier. Any caller who pays params.feeAmt in VIRTUAL tokens can invoke it. Each invocation deploys a new Genesis contract and then calls AgentFactoryV3(params.agentFactory).grantRole(BONDING_ROLE, address(addr)), granting the new Genesis contract BONDING_ROLE on the external AgentFactoryV3. The deployment script (genesis/deployGenesis.ts) confirms that FGenesis is granted DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3, enabling it to call grantRole. While the Genesis contracts themselves are constrained (their onGenesisSuccess requires FACTORY_ROLE held only by FGenesis), the BONDING_ROLE is permanently granted on AgentFactoryV3 to each newly created Genesis contract. This creates an unbounded number of BONDING_ROLE holders that cannot be easily revoked, violating least-privilege. If AgentFactoryV3 has any BONDING_ROLE-gated function beyond what is shown in IAgentFactoryV3 (which is out-of-scope), those functions become accessible to all created Genesis contracts. Furthermore, the FGenesis contract itself holds DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3 and uses it in a permissionless function, meaning any fee-paying user indirectly triggers admin-level role management on the factory.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: FGenesis.sol lines 63-82 show createGenesis with no access control modifier. Lines 79-80 show the grantRole call: `bytes32 BONDING_ROLE = AgentFactoryV3(params.agentFactory).BONDING_ROLE(); AgentFactoryV3(params.agentFactory).grantRole(BONDING_ROLE, address(addr));`. The deployment script genesis/deployGenesis.ts lines confirm: `const tx4 = await agentFactory.grantRole(await agentFactory.DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE(), deployedFGenesisAddress);` with comment 'Reason: because FGenesis Proxy need to have the rights to grant BONDING_ROLE to the new Genesis contract'. This establishes the full chain: permissionless function → deploys contract → grants privileged role on external contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control modifier (e.g., onlyRole(OPERATION_ROLE) or onlyRole(ADMIN_ROLE)). Any address that can pay the fee can trigger unlimited grantRole(BONDING_ROLE, ...) calls on AgentFactoryV3 via FGenesis's DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls FGenesis.createGenesis(gParams) with valid params and sufficient VIRTUAL token approval
3. GenesisLib.validateAndDeploy() creates and initializes a new Genesis contract
5. AgentFactoryV3(params.agentFactory).grantRole(BONDING_ROLE, addr) grants BONDING_ROLE to new Genesis
6. grantRole succeeds because FGenesis holds DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3 (per deployment script)
7. Attacker repeats to create N Genesis contracts, each with BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path is: user tx → FGenesis.createGenesis (no access control) → GenesisLib.validateAndDeploy (deploys new Genesis) → AgentFactoryV3.grantRole(BONDING_ROLE, newGenesis). FGenesis holds DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3 (confirmed by deployment script tx4), which is the role admin for BONDING_ROLE by default in OpenZeppelin AccessControl. Each iteration grants BONDING_ROLE to a new contract permanently.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: genesis/deployGenesis.ts explicitly grants DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactory to FGenesis proxy: `const tx4 = await agentFactory.grantRole(await agentFactory.DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE(), deployedFGenesisAddress);` with comment 'Reason: because FGenesis Proxy need to have the rights to grant BONDING_ROLE to the new Genesis contract'

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker with sufficient VIRTUAL tokens (params.feeAmt per call) repeatedly calls createGenesis. Each call creates a new Genesis contract and grants it BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3. While the Genesis contracts themselves cannot independently invoke AgentFactoryV3's BONDING_ROLE-gated functions (their onGenesisSuccess requires FACTORY_ROLE from FGenesis), the permanent role grants create: (1) an unbounded number of BONDING_ROLE holders that cannot be easily enumerated or revoked, (2) expanded attack surface if AgentFactoryV3 has additional BONDING_ROLE-gated functions not shown in the interface, and (3) potential for abuse if any upgrade to Genesis or AgentFactoryV3 introduces new code paths. The cost is only the fee per creation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium-High. The immediate exploitability is limited because Genesis contracts' critical functions are gated by FACTORY_ROLE. However, the defect permanently grants privileged roles on an external contract (AgentFactoryV3) without access control, creating: (1) role management complexity (cannot easily revoke BONDING_ROLE from all created contracts), (2) expanded attack surface if AgentFactoryV3 is upgraded or has additional BONDING_ROLE functions, (3) violation of least-privilege principle as any fee-payer triggers admin-level role grants on AgentFactoryV3.
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to createGenesis: restrict to OPERATION_ROLE or ADMIN_ROLE (e.g., `onlyRole(OPERATION_ROLE)`) so only authorized operators can create Genesis contracts
If permissionless creation is desired, separate the role grant from the creation: have an OPERATION_ROLE-gated function that grants BONDING_ROLE after validation
Consider granting BONDING_ROLE to FGenesis itself rather than to individual Genesis contracts, and have FGenesis relay calls to AgentFactoryV3 on behalf of Genesis contracts
Implement a mechanism to revoke BONDING_ROLE from Genesis contracts after they complete their lifecycle (success/fail/cancel)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AgentFactoryV3 mock with BONDING_ROLE getter and role tracking. 2. Deploy FGenesis with DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on the mock. 3. From an unprivileged account with sufficient VIRTUAL balance, call createGenesis multiple times. 4. Verify each created Genesis contract has BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3. 5. Enumerate role holders to confirm unbounded growth. 6. Verify no access control check prevents repeated calls.
uncertainties: AgentFactoryV3 full implementation is not in scope - unknown what other BONDING_ROLE-gated functions exist beyond initFromBondingCurve and executeBondingCurveApplication
The creation fee amount (params.feeAmt) is not known from the code - economic barrier to mass creation depends on this value

Whether this permissionless design is intentional or an oversight is unclear from code comments alone, though the deployment script comment suggests the role grant is intentional but doesn't address access control on createGenesis
additional_testing_needed: Review full AgentFactoryV3 implementation for all BONDING_ROLE-gated functions
Test mass creation of Genesis contracts to verify no gas or storage limits prevent the attack
Verify whether BONDING_ROLE can be enumerated on AgentFactoryV3 (AccessControlEnumerable vs AccessControl)
Test whether Genesis contracts can directly call AgentFactoryV3 BONDING_ROLE functions via low-level calls or fallback
